**Spotify**[[a]](#cite_note-5) is a Swedish [audio streaming](/wiki/Music_streaming_service "Music streaming service") and media service provider founded in April 2006 by [Daniel Ek](/wiki/Daniel_Ek "Daniel Ek") and [Martin Lorentzon](/wiki/Martin_Lorentzon "Martin Lorentzon").[[5]](#cite_note-6) As of December 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit), it was one of the largest providers of [music streaming services](/wiki/Music_streaming_service "Music streaming service"), with over 751 million monthly [active users](/wiki/Active_users "Active users") comprising 290 million paying subscribers.[[6]](#cite_note-About_Spotify-7) Spotify is listed on the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") in the form of [American depositary receipts](/wiki/American_depositary_receipt "American depositary receipt").

Spotify offers [DRM](/wiki/Digital_rights_management "Digital rights management")-protected audio content, including over 100 million songs and over 7 million [podcast](/wiki/Podcast "Podcast") titles, from record labels and media companies.[[6]](#cite_note-About_Spotify-7) Operating as a [freemium](/wiki/Freemium "Freemium") service, the basic features are free with advertisements and limited control, while additional features, such as offline listening and commercial-free listening, are offered via paid [subscriptions](/wiki/Subscription_business_model "Subscription business model"). Users can search for music based on artist, album, or [genre](/wiki/Music_genre "Music genre"), and can create, edit, and share [playlists](/wiki/Playlist "Playlist"). It offers some [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") features such as messaging, creating profiles, following friends, shared playlists, and creating listening parties called "Jams".

As of December 2022, Spotify is available in most of Europe, as well as Africa, the Americas, Asia, and Oceania, with availability in a total of 184 markets.[[6]](#cite_note-About_Spotify-7) Its users and subscribers are based largely in the US and Europe, jointly accounting for around 53% of users and 67% of revenue.[[7]](#cite_note-8) It has no presence in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), where the market is dominated by [QQ Music](/wiki/QQ_Music "QQ Music").[[8]](#cite_note-9) The service is available on most devices, including [Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows"), [macOS](/wiki/MacOS "MacOS"), and [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux") computers, [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") and [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") smartphones and tablets, [Smart Home](/wiki/Home_automation "Home automation") devices such as the [Amazon Echo](/wiki/Amazon_Echo "Amazon Echo") and [Google Nest](/wiki/Google_Nest "Google Nest") lines of products, and [digital media players](/wiki/Digital_media_player "Digital media player") like [Roku](/wiki/Roku "Roku").[[9]](#cite_note-10) As of December 2023, Spotify was the [47th most-visited website in the world](/wiki/List_of_most-visited_websites "List of most-visited websites") with 24.78% of its traffic coming from the United States followed by Brazil with 6.51% according to data provided by [Semrush](/wiki/Semrush "Semrush").[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12) As of 2022, Spotify is the current sponsor of Spanish football club [FC Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona"), with music artists like [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(musician) "Drake (musician)"), [Travis Scott](/wiki/Travis_Scott "Travis Scott"), and [Ed Sheeran](/wiki/Ed_Sheeran "Ed Sheeran") collaborating with the club by changing their shirts into their artist logo, sometimes used for album promotions.

Unlike physical or download sales, which pay artists a fixed price per song or album sold, Spotify pays [royalties](/wiki/Royalties "Royalties") based on the number of artist streams as a proportion of total songs streamed. It distributes approximately 70% of its total revenue to rights holders (often record labels), who then pay artists based on individual agreements.[[12]](#cite_note-:22-13) While certain musicians laud the service for offering a lawful option to combat piracy and for remunerating artists each time their music is played, others have voiced objections to Spotify's royalty structure and its effect on record sales.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Spotify was founded in 2006 in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), Sweden,[[14]](#cite_note-15) by [Daniel Ek](/wiki/Daniel_Ek "Daniel Ek"), former [chief technology officer](/wiki/Chief_technology_officer "Chief technology officer") of [Stardoll](/wiki/Stardoll "Stardoll"), and Martin Lorentzon, co-founder of [Tradedoubler](/wiki/Tradedoubler "Tradedoubler").[[15]](#cite_note-Parsons-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17) Ek first had the idea for Spotify around 2002 when peer-to-peer music service [Napster](/wiki/Napster "Napster") shut down and another illegal site [Kazaa](/wiki/Kazaa "Kazaa") became popular. Ek said he "realized that you can never legislate away from piracy. Laws can definitely help, but it doesn't take away the problem. The only way to solve the problem was to create a service that was better than piracy and at the same time compensates the music industry – that gave us Spotify."[[17]](#cite_note-auto1-18) According to Ek, the company's title was initially misheard from a name shouted by Lorentzon. Later they conceived a [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of "spot" and "identify".[[18]](#cite_note-19) Ek's initial pitch to Lorentzon was not initially related to music, but rather a way for streaming content such as video, digital films, images or music to drive advertising revenue.[[19]](#cite_note-:3-20)

### Early international launches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early international launches")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Daniel_Ek_addressing_Spotify_staff.jpg/250px-Daniel_Ek_addressing_Spotify_staff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daniel_Ek_addressing_Spotify_staff.jpg)

Daniel Ek addressing Spotify staff in 2010

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Spotify_HQ.jpg/250px-Spotify_HQ.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spotify_HQ.jpg)

Former Spotify headquarters in Stockholm

In February 2009, Spotify opened public registration for the free service tier in the United Kingdom.[[15]](#cite_note-Parsons-16) Registrations surged following the release of the mobile service, leading Spotify to halt registration for the free service in September, returning the UK to an invitation-only policy.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

Spotify launched in the United States in July 2011, and offered a six-month, ad-supported trial period, during which new users could listen to an unlimited amount of music for free. In January 2012, the free trial periods began to expire, limiting users to ten hours of streaming each month and five plays per song.[[21]](#cite_note-22) Using PC streaming, a similar structure to the one used today allowed the listener to play songs freely, but with ads every 4–7 songs depending on listening duration. Later that same year, in March, Spotify removed all limits on the free service tier indefinitely, including mobile devices.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

In April 2016, Ek and Lorentzon wrote an open letter to Swedish politicians, demanding action in three areas that they claimed hindered the company's ability to recruit top talent as Spotify grew, including access to flexible housing, better education in the programming and development fields, and stock options. Ek and Lorentzon wrote that to continue competing in a global economy, politicians needed to respond with new policies, or thousands of Spotify jobs would be moved from Sweden to the United States.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

In February 2017, Spotify announced the expansion of its United States operations in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"), New York City, at [4 World Trade Center](/wiki/4_World_Trade_Center "4 World Trade Center"), adding approximately 1,000 new jobs and retaining 832 existing positions.[[24]](#cite_note-25) The company's US headquarters are in New York City's [Flatiron District](/wiki/Flatiron_District "Flatiron District").[[25]](#cite_note-26)

On 14 November 2018, the company announced 13 new markets in the [MENA](/wiki/MENA "MENA") region, including the creation of a new Arabic hub and several playlists.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

### Other developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Other developments")]

#### Streaming records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Streaming records")]

Main articles: [List of most-streamed artists on Spotify](/wiki/List_of_most-streamed_artists_on_Spotify "List of most-streamed artists on Spotify") and [List of Spotify streaming records](/wiki/List_of_Spotify_streaming_records "List of Spotify streaming records")

In October 2015, "[Thinking Out Loud](/wiki/Thinking_Out_Loud "Thinking Out Loud")" by [Ed Sheeran](/wiki/Ed_Sheeran "Ed Sheeran") became the first song to pass 500 million streams.[[27]](#cite_note-28) A month later, Spotify announced that "[Lean On](/wiki/Lean_On "Lean On")" by [Major Lazer](/wiki/Major_Lazer "Major Lazer") and [DJ Snake](/wiki/DJ_Snake "DJ Snake") featuring [MØ](/wiki/M%C3%98 "MØ") was its most-streamed song of all time with over 525 million streams worldwide.[[28]](#cite_note-29) In April 2016, [Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") overtook [Justin Bieber](/wiki/Justin_Bieber "Justin Bieber") to become the biggest artist on Spotify, with 31.3 million monthly active listeners.[[29]](#cite_note-30) In May 2016, Rihanna was overtaken by [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(musician) "Drake (musician)") with 31.85 million monthly listeners.[[30]](#cite_note-31) In December 2016, Drake's just-under 36 million monthly listeners were overtaken by the [Weeknd](/wiki/The_Weeknd "The Weeknd")'s 36.068 million.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Later that same month, Drake's song "[One Dance](/wiki/One_Dance "One Dance")" became the first song to hit one billion streams on Spotify.[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) Upon its release in August 2017, the single "[Look What You Made Me Do](/wiki/Look_What_You_Made_Me_Do "Look What You Made Me Do")" by [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift") earned over eight million streams within 24 hours, breaking the record for the most single-day streams for a track.[[34]](#cite_note-35) On 19 June 2018, [XXXTentacion](/wiki/XXXTentacion "XXXTentacion")'s hit single "[Sad!](/wiki/Sad! "Sad!")" broke Swift's single-day streaming record, amassing 10.4 million streams the day after he was [fatally shot](/wiki/Killing_of_XXXTentacion "Killing of XXXTentacion") in Florida.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

#### User growth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: User growth")]

In March 2011, Spotify announced a customer base of 1 million paying subscribers across Europe,[[36]](#cite_note-37) and by September 2011, the number of paying subscribers had doubled to two million.[[37]](#cite_note-38) In August 2012, *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* reported 15 million active users, four million being paying Spotify subscribers.[[38]](#cite_note-39) User growth continued, reaching 20 million total active users, including five million paying customers globally and one million paying customers in the United States, in December 2012.[[39]](#cite_note-40) By March 2013, the service had 24 million active users, six million being paying subscribers,[[40]](#cite_note-41) which grew to 40 million users (including ten million paying) in May 2014,[[41]](#cite_note-42) 60 million users (including 15 million paying) in December 2014, 75 million users (20 million paying) in June 2015, 30 million paying subscribers in March 2016,[[42]](#cite_note-43) 40 million paying subscribers in September 2016,[[43]](#cite_note-44) and 100 million total users in June 2016.[[44]](#cite_note-45) In April 2020, Spotify reached 133 million premium users.[[45]](#cite_note-46) In countries affected by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), Spotify registered a fall in users in late February, but it has seen a recovery.[[46]](#cite_note-47) In March 2022, Spotify had 182 million premium subscribers.[[47]](#cite_note-48) At the end of Q2 2022, Spotify reported 188 million paying subscribers and 433 million total users.[[48]](#cite_note-49) At the end of Q3 2024, Spotify reported 252 million subscribers and 640 million monthly active users.[[49]](#cite_note-50) In February 2026, Spotify announced that it had added a record number of new users in a single quarter, adding 38 million new users in the three months to the end of December 2025 to reach 751 million monthly users.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

#### Premium-exclusive albums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Premium-exclusive albums")]

The *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")* reported in March 2017 that, as part of its efforts to renegotiate new licensing deals with music labels, Spotify and major record labels had agreed that Spotify would restrict some newly released albums to its Premium tier, with Spotify receiving a reduction in royalty fees to do so. Select albums would be available only on the Premium tier for a period of time, before general release. The deal "may be months away from being finalized, but Spotify is said to have cleared this particular clause with major record labels".[[51]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54) New reports in April confirmed that Spotify and [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group") had reached an agreement to allow artists part of Universal to limit their new album releases to the Premium service tier for a maximum of two weeks. Ek commented that "We know that not every album by every artist should be released the same way, and we've worked hard with UMG to develop a new, flexible release policy. Starting today, Universal artists can choose to release new albums on premium only for two weeks, offering subscribers an earlier chance to explore the complete creative work, while the singles are available across Spotify for all our listeners to enjoy".[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57) It was announced later in April that this type of agreement would be extended to [indie](/wiki/Indie_music_scene "Indie music scene") artists signed to the [Merlin Network](/wiki/Merlin_Network "Merlin Network") agency.[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)

#### Direct public offering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Direct public offering")]

Spotify went public on the [stock market](/wiki/Stock_market "Stock market") in April 2018 using a [direct public offering](/wiki/Direct_public_offering "Direct public offering") rather than an [initial public offering](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering"). This approach is not intended to raise fresh capital, but to let investors get their returns.[[59]](#cite_note-Spotify_23bn-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-:1-62) [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley") is the company's slated advisor on the matter.[[61]](#cite_note-:1-62)

After making its debut on the New York Stock Exchange on 3 April 2018, [CNBC](/wiki/CNBC "CNBC") reported that Spotify opened at $165.90, more than 25% above its reference price of $132.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

#### 2020 hacking incident

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2020 hacking incident")]

On 3 July 2020, [cybersecurity](/wiki/Computer_security "Computer security") firm VPNMentor discovered a database containing 380 million individual records, including the logins and passwords of Spotify users.[[63]](#cite_note-64) The database was thought to be evidence of an impending [credential stuffing](/wiki/Credential_stuffing "Credential stuffing") [cyberattack](/wiki/Cyberattack "Cyberattack") targeting Spotify[[64]](#cite_note-65) as it contained the credentials of up to 350,000 compromised user accounts.[[65]](#cite_note-66) In response to the attack, Spotify issued a rolling reset of passwords for affected accounts in November 2020.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

#### Acquisitions, podcasting and exclusivity deals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Acquisitions, podcasting and exclusivity deals")]

In May 2013, Spotify acquired music discovery app Tunigo.[[67]](#cite_note-68) In March 2014, they acquired [The Echo Nest](/wiki/The_Echo_Nest "The Echo Nest"), a music intelligence company.[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70) In June 2015, Spotify announced they had acquired Seed Scientific, a data science consulting firm and [analytics](/wiki/Analytics "Analytics") company. In a comment to [TechCrunch](/wiki/TechCrunch "TechCrunch"), Spotify said that Seed Scientific's team would lead an advanced analytics unit within the company, focused on developing data services.[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72) In January 2016, they acquired social and messaging startups Cord Project and [Soundwave](/wiki/Soundwave_(app) "Soundwave (app)"),[[72]](#cite_note-73) followed in April 2016 by CrowdAlbum, a "startup that collects photos and videos of performances shared on social networks", and would "enhance the development of products that help artists understand, activate, and monetize their audiences".[[73]](#cite_note-74) In November 2016, Spotify acquired Preact, a "cloud-based platform and service developed for companies that operate on subscription models which helps reduce churn and build up their subscriber numbers".[[74]](#cite_note-75)

In March 2017, Spotify acquired Sonalytic, an audio detection startup, for an undisclosed amount of money. Spotify stated that Sonalytic would be used to improve the company's personalized playlists, better match songs with compositions, and improve the company's publishing data system.[[75]](#cite_note-76) Later that month, Spotify also acquired MightyTV, an app connected to television streaming services, including [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") and [HBO Go](/wiki/HBO_Go "HBO Go"), that recommends content to users. Spotify intended to use MightyTV to improve its advertising efforts on the free tier of service.[[76]](#cite_note-77) In April 2017, they acquired Mediachain, a [blockchain](/wiki/Blockchain "Blockchain") startup that had been developing a [decentralized database](/wiki/Decentralized_computing "Decentralized computing") system for managing attribution, and other [metadata](/wiki/Metadata "Metadata") for media.[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79) This was followed in May 2017 with the acquisition of [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") startup Niland, which uses technology to improve personalisation and recommendation features for users.[[79]](#cite_note-80)[[80]](#cite_note-81) In November 2017, Spotify acquired [Soundtrap](/wiki/Soundtrap "Soundtrap"), an online music studio startup.[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83)

On 12 April 2018, Spotify acquired the music licensing platform Loudr.[[83]](#cite_note-84) In August 2018, Spotify bought the exclusive rights to *[The Joe Budden Podcast](/wiki/The_Joe_Budden_Podcast "The Joe Budden Podcast")* and expanded the show to a twice-weekly schedule.[[84]](#cite_note-spotify-08-18-85) On 6 February 2019, Spotify acquired the podcast networks [Gimlet Media](/wiki/Gimlet_Media "Gimlet Media") and Anchor FM Inc., with the goal of establishing themselves as a leading figure in podcasting.[[85]](#cite_note-86)[[86]](#cite_note-87)[[87]](#cite_note-:5-88) On 26 March 2019, Spotify announced they would acquire another podcast network, [Parcast](/wiki/Parcast "Parcast").[[88]](#cite_note-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90) On 12 September 2019, Spotify acquired SoundBetter, a music production marketplace for people in the music industry to collaborate on projects, and distribute music tracks for licensing.[[90]](#cite_note-91) In October 2021, SoundBetter was sold back to the founders.[[91]](#cite_note-SoundBetter-2021a-92) On 19 November 2019, Spotify announced the acquisition of the exclusive rights to *[The Last Podcast on the Left](/wiki/The_Last_Podcast_on_the_Left "The Last Podcast on the Left")*.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

On 5 February 2020, Spotify announced its intent to acquire [Bill Simmons](/wiki/Bill_Simmons "Bill Simmons")' sports and pop culture blog and podcast network [The Ringer](/wiki/The_Ringer_(website) "The Ringer (website)") for an undisclosed amount. On 19 May 2020, Spotify acquired exclusive rights to stream the popular podcast *[The Joe Rogan Experience](/wiki/The_Joe_Rogan_Experience "The Joe Rogan Experience")* beginning in September of that year, under an agreement valued at around US$100 million (equivalent to $119,100,000 in 2024).[[93]](#cite_note-94)[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-Spangler-96)[[96]](#cite_note-97)

In November 2020, Spotify announced plans to acquire [Megaphone](/wiki/Megaphone_(podcasting) "Megaphone (podcasting)") from [The Slate Group](/wiki/The_Slate_Group "The Slate Group") for US$235 million.[[97]](#cite_note-98) In March 2021, Spotify acquired app developer Betty Labs and their live social audio app, Locker Room,[[98]](#cite_note-99) On 12 May 2021. *[Armchair Expert](/wiki/Armchair_Expert "Armchair Expert")* announced on [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram") that the podcast would be available exclusively on Spotify beginning 1 July, saying they would continue to maintain the same creative control over the show after the move.[[99]](#cite_note-100)[[100]](#cite_note-101) Locker Room was rebranded in June 2021 as [Spotify Greenroom](/wiki/Spotify_Greenroom "Spotify Greenroom"), and turned into a [Clubhouse](/wiki/Clubhouse_(app) "Clubhouse (app)") competitor.[[101]](#cite_note-102) The same month, Spotify acquired Podz, a podcast discovery startup.[[102]](#cite_note-103) Also the same month, Spotify bought the exclusive rights to the *[Call Her Daddy](/wiki/Call_Her_Daddy "Call Her Daddy")* podcast.[[17]](#cite_note-auto1-18) In November 2021, Spotify acquired audiobook company Findaway, including its publishing imprint OrangeSky Audio.[[103]](#cite_note-104) In December 2021, Spotify acquired Whooshkaa, a podcast tech company that develops specialized technology that allows radio broadcasters to easily turn their existing audio content into on-demand podcast programming.[[104]](#cite_note-105)

In February 2022, Spotify acquired Chartable and Podsights. Both are podcast advertising companies.[[105]](#cite_note-106) In 2022, Spotify Greenroom rebranded as Spotify Live,[[106]](#cite_note-107) which was subsequently planned to be shut down in April 2023.[[107]](#cite_note-108) In June 2022, Spotify acquired Sonantic, a synthetic voice and video developer.[[108]](#cite_note-109) In July 2022, Spotify acquired [Heardle](/wiki/Heardle "Heardle"), a [Wordle](/wiki/Wordle "Wordle")-inspired music trivia game, for an undisclosed amount;[[109]](#cite_note-110)[[110]](#cite_note-111) Heardle was shut down in May 2023.[[111]](#cite_note-112) In October 2022, Spotify acquired the [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin")-based content moderation startup, Kinzen.[[112]](#cite_note-113) In 2023, Spotify merged Anchor into their Spotify for Podcasters tool, a rebranding move and to organize its tools for creating, managing, growing, and monetizing their content in one place.[[113]](#cite_note-auto-114) In November 2024, Spotify for Podcasters was rebranded to Spotify for Creators.[[114]](#cite_note-115) In November 2025, Spotify acquired music database [WhoSampled](/wiki/WhoSampled "WhoSampled").[[115]](#cite_note-116)

#### Company partnerships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Company partnerships")]

In January 2015, [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") announced PlayStation Music, a new music service with Spotify as its exclusive partner. PlayStation Music incorporates the Spotify service into Sony's [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3") and [PlayStation 4](/wiki/PlayStation_4 "PlayStation 4") gaming consoles, and [Sony Xperia](/wiki/Sony_Xperia "Sony Xperia") mobile devices. The service launched on 30 March 2015.[[116]](#cite_note-117) In March 2017, Spotify announced a partnership with the [South by Southwest](/wiki/South_by_Southwest "South by Southwest") (SXSW) conference for 2017, presenting specific content in special playlists through an SXSW hub in Spotify's apps. The integration also enabled Spotify within the SXSW GO app to help users discover and explore artists performing at the conference. Two more partnerships were announced in March; one with [WNYC Studios](/wiki/WNYC_Studios "WNYC Studios"), and one with [Waze](/wiki/Waze "Waze"). The WNYC Studios partnership brought various podcasts from WNYC to Spotify, including *[Note to Self](/wiki/Note_to_Self "Note to Self")*, *[On the Media](/wiki/On_the_Media "On the Media")* and *[Here's the Thing](/wiki/Here%27s_the_Thing "Here's the Thing")*. Spotify also announced that the third season of WNYC Studios' [2 Dope Queens](/wiki/2_Dope_Queens "2 Dope Queens") podcast would premiere with a two-week exclusivity period on the service on 21 March 2017.[[117]](#cite_note-118) The Waze partnership allows Waze app users to view directions to destinations within the Spotify app and access their Spotify playlists through the Waze app.[[118]](#cite_note-119)

In October 2017, [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") announced that it would be ending its [Groove Music](/wiki/Groove_Music "Groove Music") streaming service by December, with all music from users transferring to Spotify as part of a new partnership.[[119]](#cite_note-120) In December, Spotify and [Tencent](/wiki/Tencent "Tencent")'s music arm, Tencent Music Entertainment (TME), agreed to swap stakes and make an investment in each other's music businesses.[[120]](#cite_note-121)[[121]](#cite_note-122) As a result of this transaction, Spotify gained a 9% stake in TME with TME gaining a 7.5% stake in Spotify.[[122]](#cite_note-123)

In February 2018, Spotify integrated with the gaming-oriented voice chat service [Discord](/wiki/Discord_(software) "Discord (software)") on desktop clients, allowing users to display their currently playing song as a rich presence on their profile, and invite other users with Spotify Premium to group "listening parties".[[123]](#cite_note-124) In April, Spotify announced a discounted entertainment bundle with video-on-demand provider [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu"), which included discounted rates for university students.[[124]](#cite_note-125)

In May 2020, Spotify teamed up with [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") and [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") to curate podcasts around their [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan") documentary *[The Last Dance](/wiki/The_Last_Dance_(miniseries) "The Last Dance (miniseries)")*,[[125]](#cite_note-126) and in September, Spotify signed a deal with [Chernin Entertainment](/wiki/Chernin_Entertainment "Chernin Entertainment") to produce movies and TV shows.[[126]](#cite_note-127)

In 2020 and 2021, Spotify and [DC](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics"), a brand at the time under [Warner Bros. Entertainment](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") signed deals to create audio shows on the platform around characters such as Catwoman, Wonder Woman, the Riddler, Batgirl, Superman and Lois Lane, among others.[[127]](#cite_note-128)[[128]](#cite_note-129)

In 2022, Spotify became the official streaming partner of [FC Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona").[[129]](#cite_note-130)

In May 2022, Spotify announced a partnership with the online game platform and game creation system [Roblox](/wiki/Roblox "Roblox"), the partnership saw Spotify as the first streaming brand to have a presence within the game with the launch of "Spotify Island".[[130]](#cite_note-131)

In March 2023, Spotify announced a partnership with [Patreon](/wiki/Patreon "Patreon"), which Spotify claimed would "enable creators to expand their creative business through direct payments from fans, and allow fans to listen to their Patreon content on Spotify".[[113]](#cite_note-auto-114)[[131]](#cite_note-132)

On 6 October 2025, Spotify announced a partnership with [OpenAI](/wiki/OpenAI "OpenAI") to bring music and podcast recommendations inside [ChatGPT](/wiki/ChatGPT "ChatGPT"), allowing Spotify users to discover and queue new music through conversations, rather than search. Listeners will be able to link their Spotify to ChatGPT, asking it to find anything from a specific playlist to a podcast topic.[[132]](#cite_note-133)

#### Dispute with Apple

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Dispute with Apple")]

In July 2015, Spotify launched an email campaign to urge its [App Store](/wiki/App_Store_(iOS) "App Store (iOS)") subscribers to cancel their subscriptions and start new ones through its website, bypassing the 30% transaction fee for in-app purchases required for [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") applications by technology company [Apple Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.")[[133]](#cite_note-134) A later update to the Spotify app on iOS was rejected by Apple, prompting Spotify's general counsel Horacio Gutierrez to write a letter to Apple's then-general counsel [Bruce Sewell](/wiki/Bruce_Sewell "Bruce Sewell"), stating: "This latest episode raises serious concerns under both U.S. and EU competition law. It continues a troubling pattern of behavior by Apple to exclude and diminish the competitiveness of Spotify on iOS and as a rival to [Apple Music](/wiki/Apple_Music "Apple Music"), particularly when seen against the backdrop of Apple's previous anticompetitive conduct aimed at Spotify ... we cannot stand by as Apple uses the App Store approval process as a weapon to harm competitors."[[134]](#cite_note-135)

Sewell responded to the letter: "We find it troubling that you are asking for exemptions to the rules we apply to all developers and are publicly resorting to rumors and half-truths about our service." He also elaborated that "Our guidelines apply equally to all app developers, whether they are game developers, e-book sellers, video-streaming services or digital music distributors; and regardless of whether they compete against Apple. We did not alter our behavior or our rules when we introduced our own music streaming service or when Spotify became a competitor". Furthermore, he stated that "There is nothing in Apple's conduct that 'amounts to a violation of applicable antitrust laws.' Far from it. ... I would be happy to facilitate an expeditious review and approval of your app as soon as you provide us with something that is compliant with the App Store's rules".[[135]](#cite_note-136)[[136]](#cite_note-137)

In the following months, Spotify joined several other companies in filing a letter with the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union")'s [antitrust](/wiki/Antitrust "Antitrust") body indirectly accusing Apple and Google of "abusing their 'privileged position' at the top of the market", by referring to "some" companies as having "transformed into 'gatekeepers' rather than 'gateways'".[[137]](#cite_note-138)[[138]](#cite_note-139) The complaint led to the European Union announcing that it would prepare an initiative by the end of 2017 for a possible law addressing unfair competition practices.[[139]](#cite_note-140)[[140]](#cite_note-141)

Spotify released the first version of its [Apple Watch](/wiki/Apple_Watch "Apple Watch") app in November 2018, allowing playback control of the iPhone via the watch. Users can also choose which devices to play music on via Bluetooth.[[141]](#cite_note-142) In a further escalation of the dispute with Apple, on 13 March 2019, Spotify filed an antitrust complaint with the European Commission over unfair app store practices. Two days later, Apple responded, stating that the claim was misleading rhetoric and that Spotify wanted benefits of a free app without being a free app. Spotify responded with a statement calling Apple a monopolist and stated that they had only filed the complaint as Apple's actions hurt competition and consumers and clearly violated the law. It also said that Apple believed Spotify users on the app store were Apple's customers and not Spotify's.[[142]](#cite_note-143)

Apple responded to Spotify's claims by counter-claiming that Spotify's market reach and user base would not have been possible without the Apple App Store platform. Additionally, Apple stated that they have attempted to work with Spotify to integrate the service better with Apple's products, such as [Siri](/wiki/Siri "Siri") and Apple Watch.[[143]](#cite_note-144) In 2019, under [iOS 13](/wiki/IOS_13 "IOS 13"), it became possible to play Spotify music using Siri commands.[[144]](#cite_note-145)

Spotify was one of the first companies to support [Epic Games](/wiki/Epic_Games "Epic Games") in their [lawsuit against Apple](/wiki/Epic_Games_v._Apple "Epic Games v. Apple"), which was filed after Epic also tried to bypass Apple's 30% fee for [microtransactions](/wiki/Microtransactions "Microtransactions") in [Fortnite](/wiki/Fortnite "Fortnite"). In September 2020, Spotify, Epic, and other companies founded [The Coalition for App Fairness](/wiki/The_Coalition_for_App_Fairness "The Coalition for App Fairness"), which aims for better conditions for the inclusion of apps in [app stores](/wiki/App_store "App store").[[145]](#cite_note-146)

#### Dispute with Kakao Entertainment Corp.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Dispute with Kakao Entertainment Corp.")]

On 1 March 2021, Spotify confirmed that its platform would no longer have access to music from artists represented by [Kakao Entertainment](/wiki/Kakao_Entertainment "Kakao Entertainment"). However, after talking it out and renewing the contracts between the two, Spotify later announced that they had reached an agreement with Kakao Entertainment, allowing their content to be available once again on the platform across the globe.[[146]](#cite_note-147)

#### Functionality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Functionality")]

In November 2021, Spotify hid the "shuffle" button for albums following a request by singer [Adele](/wiki/Adele "Adele"), arguing that tracks in albums are supposed to be played back in the order specified by the artist to "tell a story".[[147]](#cite_note-148)

#### NFTs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: NFTs")]

In May 2022, Spotify began testing a feature that would allow select artists to promote their [NFTs](/wiki/Non-fungible_token "Non-fungible token") via their profiles. Some artists included in this initial test phase were [Steve Aoki](/wiki/Steve_Aoki "Steve Aoki") and [the Wombats](/wiki/The_Wombats "The Wombats"). The testing was very limited in nature and was only available on Spotify's Android app in the US.[[148]](#cite_note-149)

#### Artificial streams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Artificial streams")]

In May 2023, Spotify removed tens of thousands of songs, roughly 7% of the tracks uploaded by the Boomy, due to suspected "artificial streaming", the practice of using online bots to inflate the listening statistics.[[149]](#cite_note-150)

#### Ghost artists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Ghost artists")]

In 2022, the Swedish daily *[Dagens Nyheter](/wiki/Dagens_Nyheter "Dagens Nyheter")* compared Spotify streaming data against documents retrieved from the Swedish copyright collection society STIM, and found that around twenty songwriters were behind the work of more than five hundred "artists," and that thousands of their tracks were on Spotify and had been streamed millions of times.[[150]](#cite_note-Harpers-Pelly-151) In December 2024, *[Harper's Magazine](/wiki/Harper%27s_Magazine "Harper's Magazine")* released a report stating that Spotify was padding out playlists with ghost artists created by production companies in order to minimise royalty costs and increase profits. According to the report, the practice started in 2017 with a program called Perfect Fit Content (PFC).[[151]](#cite_note-152)[[150]](#cite_note-Harpers-Pelly-151)

#### Donald Trump donation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Donald Trump donation")]

In 2025, Spotify donated $150,000 to President Donald Trump's [inauguration ceremony](/wiki/Second_inauguration_of_Donald_Trump "Second inauguration of Donald Trump"), as well as hosted an inauguration-related brunch.[[152]](#cite_note-153)[[153]](#cite_note-154)

#### DJ software

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: DJ software")]

Spotify supports integration with DJ software, allowing [DJs](/wiki/Disc_jockey "Disc jockey") to [mix](/wiki/DJ_mix "DJ mix") with music streamed from the platform.[[154]](#cite_note-:4-155) This integration was removed in July 2020[[155]](#cite_note-156) but was reintroduced in September 2025, now supporting [Serato](/wiki/Serato "Serato"), [Rekordbox](/wiki/Rekordbox "Rekordbox"), and [Djay](/wiki/Djay_(software) "Djay (software)").[[154]](#cite_note-:4-155) Though all three applications support real-time [stem separation](/wiki/Stem_separation "Stem separation") and mixing, this functionality is disabled on music streamed from Spotify.[[154]](#cite_note-:4-155) Caching tracks for offline use is also unsupported in DJ software.[[154]](#cite_note-:4-155)

#### 2025 hacking incident

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 2025 hacking incident")]

In December 2025, over 300 [terabytes](/wiki/Byte#Multiple-byte_units "Byte") of files were scraped from Spotify's servers and uploaded to [Anna's Archive](/wiki/Anna%27s_Archive "Anna's Archive"), totalling 86 million songs uploaded from 2007 to July 2025 that account for 37% of all songs and 99.6% of all listens, as well as 256 million rows of song [metadata](/wiki/Metadata "Metadata"). Spotify responded to the hacking, condeming it and stating that they have implemented new safeguards against similar attacks and banned user accounts believed to be connected to the leak.[[156]](#cite_note-157)[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159)

## Unionization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Unionization")]

Spotify recognizes [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") at its US podcasting subsidiaries [The Ringer](/wiki/The_Ringer_(website) "The Ringer (website)") and [Spotify Studios](/wiki/Spotify_Studios "Spotify Studios") since 2019. In Germany, a [works council](/wiki/Works_council "Works council") was established in 2023. Swedish trade unions have unsuccessfully attempted to [bargain collectively](/wiki/Collective_bargaining "Collective bargaining") with Spotify since 2023.

### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Germany")]

Spotify [GmbH](/wiki/GmbH "GmbH") employees in Berlin established an [electoral board](/wiki/Electoral_board_(works_council) "Electoral board (works council)") in February 2023,[[162]](#cite_note-164) which prepared the election for the [works council](/wiki/Works_council "Works council") in April.[[163]](#cite_note-165)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*]

### Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Sweden")]

Spotify [AB](/wiki/Aktiebolag "Aktiebolag") does not recognize any trade unions or have any [collective agreements](/wiki/Collective_agreements "Collective agreements") in Sweden. Spotify ended joint-negotiations with the three trade unions [Unionen](/wiki/Unionen "Unionen"), Akavia, and [Engineers of Sweden](/wiki/Engineers_of_Sweden "Engineers of Sweden") (both affiliates of [SACO](/wiki/Swedish_Confederation_of_Professional_Associations "Swedish Confederation of Professional Associations")) in August 2023. The three unions petitioned Spotify to negotiate back in May. 90% of Swedish workers are [covered by collective agreements](/wiki/Collective_agreement_coverage "Collective agreement coverage"). In tech companies bargaining coverage is less common. Swedish labor disputes are also happening at [Tesla](/wiki/Tesla_and_unions#Sweden "Tesla and unions") and [Klarna](/wiki/Klarna "Klarna") as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit).[[164]](#cite_note-166)[[165]](#cite_note-167)

In November 2022, Henry Catalini Smith, a Spotify engineer in [Malmö](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6 "Malmö"), set up the channel #kollektivavtal in the internal company [Slack](/wiki/Slack_Technologies "Slack Technologies"), which means "[collective agreement](/wiki/Collective_agreement "Collective agreement")" in Swedish. The channel grew to 2,000 participants. 700 employees have since joined Unionen, with another 100 each joining Engineers of Sweden and Akavia.[[166]](#cite_note-168) Catalini Smith no longer works at Spotify.[[167]](#cite_note-169)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: United States")]

[Writers Guild of America, East](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America,_East "Writers Guild of America, East") represents two union affiliates at [Spotify Studios](/wiki/Spotify_Studios "Spotify Studios") and [The Ringer](/wiki/The_Ringer_(website) "The Ringer (website)").[[168]](#cite_note-:0-170)

The United Musicians and Allied Workers campaigns for a fairer redistribution and compensation system for musicians.[[169]](#cite_note-UMAW-171) The United Musicians and Allied Workers (UMAW) was established in 2020 during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). One year later, in 31 cities worldwide, 27,500 musicians joined UMAW's campaign #JusticeAtSpotify to demand a compensation of one cent per audio [stream](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media"). Moreover, they are asking for a fairer redistribution system, as smaller artists are disproportionately disadvantaged on Spotify.[[170]](#cite_note-172)[[169]](#cite_note-UMAW-171)

One month after Spotify acquired [Gimlet Media](/wiki/Gimlet_Media "Gimlet Media") in February 2019, 75% of staff at Gimlet Media went public, signing [union cards](/wiki/Card_check "Card check") and seeking [voluntary recognition](/wiki/Voluntary_recognition "Voluntary recognition").[[171]](#cite_note-173)[[172]](#cite_note-174) In August, [The Ringer's](/wiki/The_Ringer_(website) "The Ringer (website)") editorial staff voted to unionize with the [Writers Guild of America, East](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America,_East "Writers Guild of America, East") (before it was owned by Spotify). The union was voluntarily recognized by Ringer management four days later.[[173]](#cite_note-175) In February 2020, Spotify announced it was acquiring The Ringer, and inheriting the previously established union.[[174]](#cite_note-:2-176)[[175]](#cite_note-177) A year later, in April 2021, writers and producers ratified their first [collective agreement](/wiki/Collective_agreement "Collective agreement") with Gimlet Media and Ringer. It would last 3 years, with minimum base salary of $57,000 for Ringer staff and $73,000 for Gimlet producers. There was no provision regarding worker ownership of content created, one of the initial demands.[[176]](#cite_note-178)[[177]](#cite_note-179)

Spotify acquired [Parcast](/wiki/Parcast "Parcast") in March 2019.[[178]](#cite_note-180) Six months later, Parcast workers went public with their union drive,[[179]](#cite_note-181) which was recognized a month later by Parcast.[[180]](#cite_note-Parcast_Union-182) After 15 months of bargaining, the Parcast union consisting of 56 workers ratified their first collective agreement, which included a minimum salary of $70,000, annual increases and [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action "Affirmative action") while hiring.[[180]](#cite_note-Parcast_Union-182)

In March 2024, The Writers Guild of America, East ratified a collective agreement with Ringer and Spotify Studios (Spotify Studios was formed as a merger of [Gimlet Media](/wiki/Gimlet_Media "Gimlet Media"), [Parcast](/wiki/Parcast "Parcast") and their respective unions) which increased minimum base salaries to $65,000, protections for migrant employees and included safe-guards against usage of [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") to create "digital replicas" of their voices.[[168]](#cite_note-:0-170)

## Business model

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Business model")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Spotify_HK_Stockholm.jpg/250px-Spotify_HK_Stockholm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spotify_HK_Stockholm.jpg)

Spotify offices in Stockholm, 2024

Spotify operates under a [freemium](/wiki/Freemium "Freemium") business model (basic services are free, while additional features are offered via paid subscriptions). Spotify generates revenue by selling premium streaming subscriptions to users and advertising placements to third parties. Some of the premium options users may choose from include individual, duo, family, and student.[[181]](#cite_note-183)

In December 2013, the company launched a new website, "Spotify for Artists", explaining its business model and revenue data. Spotify gets its content from major record labels as well as [independent artists](/wiki/Independent_artist "Independent artist") and pays [copyright](/wiki/Copyright "Copyright") holders [royalties](/wiki/Royalties "Royalties") for streaming music. The company pays about 70% of its total revenue to rights holders.[[182]](#cite_note-Spotify_business-184) Of that amount, about 58.5% of its total revenue goes to the owners of [sound recording copyrights](/wiki/Sound_recording_copyright "Sound recording copyright"), and the other 12% goes to the owners of [musical compositions](/wiki/Musical_composition "Musical composition"). In the United States, this is split between 6% mechanical royalties (paid via the [Mechanical Licensing Collective](/wiki/Mechanical_Licensing_Collective "Mechanical Licensing Collective")) and 6% performance royalties (paid via [performing rights organizations](/wiki/Performing_rights_organization "Performing rights organization") or PROs).[[183]](#cite_note-manatt-2016-185)[[184]](#cite_note-spotify-mlc-186)[[c]](#cite_note-187)

Spotify for Artists states that the company does not have a fixed per-play rate; instead, it considers factors such as the user's home country and the individual artist's royalty rate. Rights holders received an average per-play payout between $.000029 and $.0084.[[182]](#cite_note-Spotify_business-184)

Today, [royalties](https://support.spotify.com/us/artists/article/understanding-spotify-royalties/) on all streaming services, including Spotify are paid on a per user basis not per stream as this allows the artists who users listen to the most to receive the largest percentage of the payouts. In 2013, Spotify revealed that it paid artists an average of $0.007 per stream. *[Music Week](/wiki/Music_Week "Music Week")* editor Tim Ingham commented that while the figure may "initially seem alarming", he noted: "Unlike buying a CD or download, streaming is not a one-off payment. Hundreds of millions of streams of tracks are happening every day, which quickly multiplies the potential revenues on offer—and is a constant long-term source of income for artists."[[185]](#cite_note-188) According to [Ben Sisario](/wiki/Ben_Sisario "Ben Sisario") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, approximately 13,000 out of seven million artists (0.19%) on Spotify generated $50,000 (equivalent to $59,540 in 2024) or more in payments in 2020.[[186]](#cite_note-189)

In November 2023, Spotify announced a new royalty model taking effect in 2024, aiming to reduce the amount of "fraudulent" royalties collected from "functional" non-music tracks with short lengths (such as environmental sounds and [white noise](/wiki/White_noise "White noise")). Under the model, all royalties go to songs with at least 1,001 listens. Meaning that tracks must reach at least 1,000 listens in 12 months as well as a minimum number of unique listeners to become eligible for sound recording royalties, "functional" tracks will require a longer amount of play time to count as a listen, and distributors will face reprimands if their content is responsible for generating "fraudulent" royalties. This eligibility rule only applies to royalties for sound recordings, not musical compositions.[[187]](#cite_note-spotify-track-eligibility-190) The changes faced a mixed reaction from the music industry, who believed that it would be detrimental to emerging musicians, but would make a larger share of total royalty payments available to musicians.[[188]](#cite_note-191)[[189]](#cite_note-192)[[190]](#cite_note-193)

### Accounts and subscriptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Accounts and subscriptions")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

As of August 2022, the two Spotify subscription tiers were:

Spotify subscription tiers

| Type | Remove ads | Mobile listening | Sound quality | Listen offline | Spotify Connect |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Spotify Free | No | Limited (shuffle-only mode) | Up to 160 kbit/s [Vorbis](/wiki/Vorbis "Vorbis") or 128 kbit/s [Advanced Audio Coding (AAC)](/wiki/Advanced_Audio_Coding "Advanced Audio Coding") for the web player | No | Limited (Spotify Connect device using the new [SDK](/wiki/Software_development_kit "Software development kit")) |
| Spotify Premium | Yes\* | Yes | Up to 320 kbit/s Vorbis or 256 kbit/s AAC for the web player | Yes | Yes |

\*Still shows sponsored content, such as *Spotify Showcase* banners.[[191]](#cite_note-showcase-spotify-for-artists-194)

None of these subscriptions limit listening time.

In March 2014, Spotify introduced a discounted Premium subscription tier for students in which students in the United States enrolled in a university pay half-price for a Premium subscription.[[192]](#cite_note-195) In April 2017, the discount was expanded to 33 more countries.[[193]](#cite_note-196)[[194]](#cite_note-197)

Spotify introduced a Family subscription in October 2014, which allows up to 5 family members to have a premium subscription.[[195]](#cite_note-198)[[196]](#cite_note-199) In May 2016, the limit was changed to 6 family members, and the price was reduced.[[197]](#cite_note-200) The Family subscription provides access to [Spotify Kids](/wiki/Spotify_Kids "Spotify Kids").[[198]](#cite_note-:12-201)

In November 2018, Spotify announced it was opening up Spotify Connect to all of the users using its Free service, however, these changes still required products supporting Spotify Connect to support the latest [software development kit](/wiki/Software_development_kit "Software development kit").[[199]](#cite_note-202)[[200]](#cite_note-203)

In July 2020,[[201]](#cite_note-204) Spotify added another tier, Premium Duo.[[202]](#cite_note-205) This is aimed at couples and it lets up to two people (living at the same address) share a subscription.

In February 2021, Spotify announced their plans to introduce a HiFi subscription, to offer listening in [high fidelity](/wiki/High_fidelity "High fidelity"), lossless sound quality.[[203]](#cite_note-HiFi-206) On 10 January 2022, the HiFi tier was delayed indefinitely. On 10 September 2025, it was announced that Lossless would be rolling out to Premium listeners gradually to more than 50 markets through October.[[204]](#cite_note-207)

In August 2021, Spotify launched a test subscription tier called Spotify Plus. The subscription costs $0.99 and is supposed to be a combination of the free and premium tiers. Subscribers to this plan will still receive ads but will get the ability to listen to songs without shuffle mode and skip any number of tracks. The company reported that the tier conditions may change before its full launch.[[205]](#cite_note-208)

In September 2025, Spotify began removing restrictions preventing free users from listening to specific tracks. Mobile listeners are now able to tap on any song or search for what they’d like to play. This was previously limited to desktop and iPad users only.[[206]](#cite_note-209)

| Individual | $12.99 |

Spotify also offers an "Audiobook Access" option giving paying subscribers access to their [audiobook](/wiki/Audiobook "Audiobook") catalog for a limited time each month.[[207]](#cite_note-SpotifyLevels-210)

### Monetization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Monetization")]

In 2008, just after launch, the company made a loss of 31.8 million Swedish kronor (US$4.4 million).[[208]](#cite_note-211) In October 2010, *[Wired](/wiki/Wired_(website) "Wired (website)")* reported that Spotify was making more money for labels in Sweden than any other retailer "online or off".[[209]](#cite_note-212) Years after growth and expansion, a November 2012 report suggested strong momentum for the company. In 2011, it reported a near US$60 million net loss from revenue of $244 million (equivalent to $334,700,000 in 2024), while it was expected to generate a net loss of $40 million (equivalent to $53,860,000 in 2024) from revenue of $500 million in 2012 (equivalent to $673,200,000 in 2024).[[210]](#cite_note-213) Another source of income was music purchases from within the app, however this service was removed in January 2013.[[211]](#cite_note-No_purchase-214)

In May 2016, Spotify announced "Sponsored Playlists", a monetisation opportunity in which brands can specify the audiences they have in mind, with Spotify matching the marketer with suitable music in a playlist.[[212]](#cite_note-215)[[213]](#cite_note-216) That September, Spotify announced that it had paid a total of over $5 billion to the music industry. In June 2017, as part of renegotiated licenses with Universal Music Group and Merlin Network, Spotify's financial filings revealed its agreement to pay more than $2 billion (equivalent to $2,510,000,000 in 2024) in minimum payments over the next two years.[[214]](#cite_note-217)[[215]](#cite_note-218)[[216]](#cite_note-219)

As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit), Spotify was not yet a profitable company.[[217]](#cite_note-220) Spotify's revenue for Q1 2020 amounted to €1.85 billion ($2 billion). A large part of this sum, €1.7 billion ($1.84 billion), came from Spotify Premium subscribers. Gross profit in the same quarter amounted to €472 million ($511 million), with an operating loss of €17 million ($18 million).[[218]](#cite_note-221) Despite subscriber and podcasts growth, during Q2 of 2020, Spotify reported a loss of €356 million (€1.91 per share). The "deeper" loss came as a result of the company's tax debt to over one-third of its employees in Sweden.[[219]](#cite_note-222) Spotify became profitable for the first time in 2024 with a net profit of €1.14 billion ($1.17 billion).[[220]](#cite_note-223)[[221]](#cite_note-224)

By 2024, Spotify added 15 hours of audiobook listening to its premium tier. In order to balance the expense of licensing audiobooks and music together, the company started a new "bundle" royalty rate to songwriters. [Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)") estimated that the changes will pay songwriters and publishers $150 million less "from premium, duo and family plans for the first 12 months that this is in effect, compared to what they would have earned if these three subscriptions were never bundled."[[222]](#cite_note-Billboard-225)

#### Funding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Funding")]

In February 2010, Spotify received a small investment from [Founders Fund](/wiki/Founders_Fund "Founders Fund"), where board member [Sean Parker](/wiki/Sean_Parker "Sean Parker") was recruited to assist Spotify in "winning the labels over in the world's largest music market". In June 2011, Spotify secured $100 million of funding (equivalent to $137,200,000 in 2024) and planned to use this to support its US launch.[[223]](#cite_note-226) The new round of funding valued the company at $1 billion.[[224]](#cite_note-227) A [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs")-led round of funding closed in November 2012, raising around $100 million (equivalent to $134,600,000 in 2024) at a $3 billion valuation (equivalent to $4,039,000,000 in 2024). In April 2015, Spotify began another round of fundraising, with a report from *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* stating it was seeking $400 million (equivalent to $515,700,000 in 2024), which would value the company at $8.4 billion (equivalent to $10,831,000,000 in 2024). The financing was closed in June 2015, with Spotify raising $526 million (equivalent to $678,200,000 in 2024), at a value of $8.53 billion (equivalent to $10,998,000,000 in 2024).[[225]](#cite_note-228) In January 2016, Spotify raised another $500 million (equivalent to $638,600,000 in 2024) through convertible bonds. In March 2016, Spotify raised $1 billion (equivalent to $1,277,000,000 in 2024) in financing by debt plus a discount of 20% on shares once the [initial public offering](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering") (IPO) of shares takes place.[[226]](#cite_note-229) The company was, according to *[TechCrunch](/wiki/TechCrunch "TechCrunch")*, planning to launch on the [stock market](/wiki/Stock_market "Stock market") in 2017, but in 2017 it was seen as planning on doing the IPO in 2018 to "build up a better balance sheet and work on shifting its business model to improve its margins".[[227]](#cite_note-tc-ipo-230)

### Downloads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Downloads")]

In March 2009, Spotify began offering [music downloads](/wiki/Music_download "Music download") in the United Kingdom, France, and Spain. Users could purchase tracks from Spotify, which partnered with [7digital](/wiki/7digital "7digital") to incorporate the feature. The ability to purchase and download music tracks via the app was removed on 4 January 2013.[[211]](#cite_note-No_purchase-214)

### Spotify for Artists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Spotify for Artists")]

In November 2015, Spotify introduced a "Fan Insights" panel in limited [beta](/wiki/Software_release_life_cycle "Software release life cycle") form, letting artists and managers access data on monthly listeners, geographical data, demographic information, music preferences and more.[[228]](#cite_note-231) In April 2017, the panel was upgraded to leave beta status, renamed as "Spotify for Artists", and opened to all artists and managers. Additional features include the ability to get "verified" status with a blue checkmark on an artist's profile, receiving artist support from Spotify, customising the profile page with photos and promoting a certain song as their "pick".[[229]](#cite_note-232)[[230]](#cite_note-233)

In September 2018, Spotify announced "Upload Beta", allowing artists to upload directly to the platform instead of going through a [distributor](/wiki/Music_industry "Music industry") or record label.[[231]](#cite_note-234) The feature was rolled out to a small number of US-based artists by invitation only. Uploading was free and artists received 100% of the revenue from songs they uploaded; artists were able to control when their release went public. On 1 July 2019, Spotify deprecated the program and announced plans to stop accepting direct uploads by the end of that month and eventually remove all content uploaded in this manner.[[232]](#cite_note-235)

### Industry initiatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Industry initiatives")]

In June 2017, *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* reported that Spotify would announce "Secret Genius", a new initiative aimed at highlighting songwriters and producers, and the effect those people have on the music industry and the artists' careers. The project, which would feature awards, "Songshops" songwriting workshops, curated playlists, and podcasts, is an effort to "shine a light on these people behind the scenes who play such a big role in some of the most important moments of our lives. When the general public hears a song, they automatically associate it with the artist who sings it, not the people behind the scenes who make it happen, so we thought the title Secret Genius was appropriate", Spotify's former Global Head of Creator Services [Troy Carter](/wiki/Troy_Carter_(talent_manager) "Troy Carter (talent manager)") told *Variety* the first awards ceremony would take place in late 2017,[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] and was intended to honour "the top songwriters, producers and publishers in the industry as well as up-and-coming talent". Additionally, as part of "The Ambassador Program", 13 songwriters would each host a Songshop workshop, in which their peers would collaboratively attempt to create a hit song, with the first workshop taking place in Los Angeles in June 2017.[[233]](#cite_note-236)

In October 2017, Spotify launched "Rise", a program aimed at promoting emerging artists.[[234]](#cite_note-237)[[235]](#cite_note-238) In February 2020, Spotify announced it would be featuring new songwriter pages and 'written by' playlists. This was aimed at giving fans a behind the scenes look at the process of some of their favorite songwriters. Initial pages added included Justin Trantor, [Meghan Trainor](/wiki/Meghan_Trainor "Meghan Trainor"), and [Missy Elliott](/wiki/Missy_Elliott "Missy Elliott"). Spotify thereafter announced it was planning to add more of these pages and playlists to highlight songwriters.[[236]](#cite_note-239)[[237]](#cite_note-240)

In January 2021, Spotify made a selection of audiobooks available on the platform as a test of developing a greater breadth of content for users.[[238]](#cite_note-241) The addition of audiobooks to the service would create similar offerings to that of Amazon's [Audible](/wiki/Audible_(service) "Audible (service)"). In 2020, Spotify partnered with [Wizarding World](/wiki/Wizarding_World "Wizarding World") to release a series of recorded readings of *Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone,* by various stars of the franchise.[[239]](#cite_note-242) In November 2023, Spotify expanded free access to 200,000 audiobooks for Spotify Premium subscribers.[[240]](#cite_note-243) In April 2024, Spotify expanded access to the audiobooks from the US, UK and Australia to include Canada, Ireland and New Zealand. The company also announced an expansion of its book catalogue to 250,000 books.[[241]](#cite_note-244)[[222]](#cite_note-Billboard-225)

In recent years, Spotify has awarded plaques to artists when their songs reach a billion streams on the platform.[[242]](#cite_note-245)[[243]](#cite_note-246)[[244]](#cite_note-247)

### Stations by Spotify

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Stations by Spotify")]

On 31 January 2018, Spotify started testing a new [Pandora](/wiki/Pandora_Radio "Pandora Radio")-styled standalone app called Stations by Spotify for Australian Android users.[[245]](#cite_note-248) It featured 62 music channels, each devoted to a particular genre. Spotify itself has two channels named after its playlists that link directly to the users' profile: "Release Radar" and "Discover Weekly". The aim was to help users to listen to the music they want without [information overload](/wiki/Information_overload "Information overload") or spending time building their own playlists. At launch, the skipping feature was not featured to "reinforce the feel of radio", but it was quietly added later and with no limits. Songs can be "loved" but cannot be "hated". If a song is "loved", a custom radio channel will be created based on it, and when there are at least 15 of these songs, a "My Favourites" channel is unlocked.

The standalone app was made available to all iOS and Android users in the United States since 4 June 2019.[[246]](#cite_note-249)

Spotify announced the app would be shut down on 16 May 2022. The company said users would be able to login into the main Spotify app with their Stations account and transfer their stations into Spotify.[[247]](#cite_note-250)
