Spotify has client software currently available for [Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows"), [macOS](/wiki/MacOS "MacOS"), [Wear OS](/wiki/Wear_OS "Wear OS"), [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)"), [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS"), [watchOS](/wiki/WatchOS "WatchOS"), [iPadOS](/wiki/IPadOS "IPadOS"), [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3"), [PlayStation 4](/wiki/PlayStation_4 "PlayStation 4"), [PlayStation 5](/wiki/PlayStation_5 "PlayStation 5"), [Xbox One](/wiki/Xbox_One "Xbox One") and [Xbox Series X/S](/wiki/Xbox_Series_X_and_Series_S "Xbox Series X and Series S") game consoles. There is an official, although unsupported Linux version. Spotify also offers a proprietary protocol known as "Spotify Connect", which lets users listen to music through a wide range of entertainment systems, including speakers, receivers, TVs, cars, and smartwatches. Spotify also has a web player (open.spotify.com).[[256]](#cite_note-Web_player-259) Offline Music listening is possible on watchOS[[257]](#cite_note-260) and more recently added to Google's WearOS[[258]](#cite_note-261) for those with premium subscriptions. Unlike the apps, the web player does not have the ability to download music for offline listening. In June 2017, Spotify became available as an app through [Windows Store](/wiki/Windows_Store "Windows Store").[[259]](#cite_note-262)[[260]](#cite_note-263)

### Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Features")]

[![It is showing an album from Kevin MacLeod, and also the playlist queue, as the playlist queue is no longer fullscreen when opened. It's now a sidebar.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a6/Spotify_Screenshot_Windows.png/250px-Spotify_Screenshot_Windows.png)](/wiki/File:Spotify_Screenshot_Windows.png)

Spotify desktop client running on Windows

In Spotify's apps, music can be browsed or searched for via various parameters, such as artist, album, genre, [playlist](/wiki/Playlist "Playlist"), or record label. Users can create, edit and share playlists, share tracks on social media, and make playlists with other users. Spotify provides access to over 100 million songs, 7 million podcasts, and 4 billion playlists. In June 2012, Soundrop became the first Spotify app to attract major funding, receiving $3 million (equivalent to $4,039,000 in 2024) from Spotify investor Northzone.[[261]](#cite_note-264)[[262]](#cite_note-265)[[6]](#cite_note-About_Spotify-7)

In November 2011, Spotify introduced a Spotify Apps service that made it possible for third-party developers to design applications that could be hosted within the Spotify computer software. The applications provided features such as synchronised lyrics, music reviews, and song recommendations.[[263]](#cite_note-266)[[264]](#cite_note-267)[[265]](#cite_note-268) However, after the June 2014 announcement of a Web [API](/wiki/API "API") that allowed third-party developers to integrate Spotify content in their own web applications, the company discontinued its Spotify Apps platform in October, stating that its new development tools for the Spotify web player fulfilled many of the advantages of the former Spotify Apps service that allows users to access the service directly from their web browser without downloading the app.[[266]](#cite_note-269)

In April 2012, Spotify introduced a "Spotify Play Button", an embeddable music player that can be added to blogs, websites, or social media profiles, that lets visitors listen to a specific song, playlist, or album without leaving the page.[[267]](#cite_note-270) The following November, the company began rolling out a web player, with a similar design to its computer programs, but without the requirement of any installation.[[256]](#cite_note-Web_player-259)

In December 2012, Spotify introduced a "Follow" tab and a "Discover" tab,[[268]](#cite_note-271) along with a "Collection" section.[[269]](#cite_note-272) "Follow" lets users follow artists and friends to see what they are listening to, while "Discover" directs users to new releases as well as music, review, and concert recommendations based on listening history. Users can add tracks to a "Collection" section of the app, rather than adding them to a specific playlist.[[270]](#cite_note-verge-collection-273)[[271]](#cite_note-274) The features were announced by CEO Daniel Ek at a press conference, with Ek saying that a common user complaint about the service was that "Spotify is great when you know what music you want to listen to, but not when you don't".[[272]](#cite_note-275)

In May 2015, Spotify announced a new "Home" start-page that could recommend music. The company also introduced "Spotify Running", a feature aimed at improving music while running with music matched to running tempo, and announced that [podcasts](/wiki/Podcast "Podcast") and videos ("entertainment, news and clips") would be coming to Spotify, along with "Spotify Originals" content.[[273]](#cite_note-276)[[274]](#cite_note-277)[[275]](#cite_note-278)

In December 2015, Spotify debuted [Spotify Wrapped](/wiki/Spotify_Wrapped "Spotify Wrapped"), a program that creates playlists based on each user's most listened-to songs from the year. Users then can view and save this playlist at the end of the year.[[276]](#cite_note-279)[[277]](#cite_note-280)

In January 2016, Spotify and music annotation service [Genius](/wiki/Genius_(website) "Genius (website)") formed a partnership, bringing annotation information from Genius into infocards presented while songs are playing in Spotify. The functionality is limited to selected playlists and was only available on Spotify's iOS app at launch,[[278]](#cite_note-281)[[279]](#cite_note-282)[[280]](#cite_note-283) being expanded to the Android app in April 2017. This feature was known as "Behind the Lyrics".[[281]](#cite_note-284)[[282]](#cite_note-285) As of 18 November 2021,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit) "Behind the Lyrics" has been replaced with auto-generated real-time lyrics due to consumer demand. The feature is powered by lyrics providers [Musixmatch](/wiki/Musixmatch "Musixmatch")[[283]](#cite_note-286) and [PetitLyrics](/w/index.php?title=PetitLyrics&action=edit&redlink=1 "PetitLyrics (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%83%97%E3%83%81%E3%83%AA%E3%83%AA "ja:プチリリ")] (only in Japan).

In May 2017, Spotify introduced Spotify Codes for its mobile apps, a way for users to share specific artists, tracks, playlists or albums with other people. Users find the relevant content to share and press a "soundwave-style barcode" on the display. A camera icon in the apps' search fields lets other users point their device's camera at the code, which takes them to the same content.[[284]](#cite_note-287)[[285]](#cite_note-288)[[286]](#cite_note-289)

In January 2019, Spotify introduced Car View for Android, allowing devices running Android to have a compact Now Playing screen when the device is connected to a car's Bluetooth.[[287]](#cite_note-290)[[288]](#cite_note-291) Also in January 2019, Spotify beta-tested its Canvas feature, where artists or labels can upload looping 3 to 8-second moving visuals to their tracks, replacing album covers in the "Now Playing" view; users have the option to turn off this feature. Canvas is only available for Spotify's iOS and Android mobile apps.[[289]](#cite_note-292) Months later, Spotify tested its own version of stories (the sharing format popularized by social apps) known as "Storyline", and the focus is on allowing artists to share their own insights, inspiration, details about their creative process or other meanings behind the music.[[290]](#cite_note-293)

In March 2021, Spotify announced an upcoming option for higher-resolution sound, Spotify Hi-Fi.[[291]](#cite_note-294)

In September 2025, Lossless audio, which allows Spotify users to listen to songs in very high quality, became available through Spotify [premium](/wiki/Premium_(marketing) "Premium (marketing)").[[292]](#cite_note-295)

#### Playlists and discovery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Playlists and discovery")]

In July 2015, Spotify launched Discover Weekly, a playlist generated weekly. Updated on Mondays, it provides users with music recommendations.[[293]](#cite_note-296)[[294]](#cite_note-297) In December 2015, *[Quartz](/wiki/Quartz_(publication) "Quartz (publication)")* reported that songs in Discover Weekly playlists had been streamed 1.7 billion times.[[295]](#cite_note-298)

In March 2016, Spotify launched six playlists branded as Fresh Finds, including the main playlist and Fire Emoji, Basement, Hiptronix, Six Strings, and Cyclone (hip-hop, electronic, pop, guitar-driven, and experimental music respectively). The playlists spotlight songs by lesser-known musicians and their songs.[[296]](#cite_note-299)

In August 2016, Spotify launched Release Radar, a personalized playlist that allows users to stay up-to-date on new music released by the artists they listen to the most. It also helps users discover new music, by mixing in other artists' music. The playlist is updated every Friday, and is a maximum of two hours in length.[[297]](#cite_note-300)[[298]](#cite_note-301)

Spotify provides artists taking part in RADAR with resources and access to integrated marketing opportunities to help them boost their careers, in addition to expanded reach and exposure to 178 markets worldwide.[[299]](#cite_note-302) In 2016, Spotify introduced its Daily Mix feature, which creates playlists of music that a user has previously listened to on the platform.[[300]](#cite_note-303) In 2017, Spotify introduced RapCaviar, a hip-hop playlist.[[301]](#cite_note-304)[[302]](#cite_note-305) Rap Caviar had 10.9 million followers by 2019, becoming one of Spotify's Top 5 playlists.[[303]](#cite_note-306) RapCaviar was originally curated by Tuma Basa.[[304]](#cite_note-307) It was relaunched by Carl Chery in 2019.[[305]](#cite_note-308)

In June 2019, Spotify launched a custom playlist titled "Your Daily Drive" that closely replicates the [drive time](/wiki/Drive_time "Drive time") format of many traditional radio stations. It combines short-form podcast news updates from *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")*, [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR"), and [PRI](/wiki/Public_Radio_International "Public Radio International") with a mix of a user's favorite songs and artists interspersed with tracks the listener has yet to discover. "Your Daily Drive", which is found in a user's library under the "Made For You" section, updates throughout the day.[[306]](#cite_note-309) In May 2020, Spotify introduced the Group Session feature. This feature allows two or more Premium users in the same location to share control over the music that is being played.[[307]](#cite_note-310) The Group Session feature was later expanded to allow any Premium user to join/participate in a Group Session, with a special link the host can send to participants.[[308]](#cite_note-311)

In July 2021, Spotify launched the "What's New" feed, a section that collects all new releases and episodes from artists and podcasts that the user follows. The feature is represented by a bell icon on the app's main page and is available on iOS and Android.[[309]](#cite_note-312)

In November 2021, Spotify launched the City and Local Pulse charts, aimed at representing the songs listened to in major cities around the world. The charts are available for 200 cities with the most listeners on Spotify.[[310]](#cite_note-313)

In 2023, Spotify launched additional features to help independent artists distributing their music on the platform reach a wider array of potential fans. One such feature is a tool that Spotify rolled out in March 2023, called "Discovery Mode". Discovery Mode allows artists who meet certain criteria and have a Spotify For Artists account to submit qualifying songs for Spotify's in-house promotion services. Spotify helps place songs campaigned through Discovery Mode on listeners' personal algorithmic playlists. Discovery Mode does not require an upfront budget. Instead, a 30% commission is applied to recording royalties generated from all streams of selected songs in Discovery Mode contexts—Spotify Radio and Autoplay. All other streams of selected songs outside of Spotify Radio and Autoplay remain commission-free.[[311]](#cite_note-314) In September 2023, Spotify introduced its latest playlist update 'Daylist', a new kind of playlist which adapts to the user's mood throughout the day.[[312]](#cite_note-315)[[313]](#cite_note-316)

In April 2025, Spotify expanded its AI Playlist beta feature to Premium listeners in over 40 new English-speaking markets, including countries in Africa, Asia, Europe, and the Caribbean. The tool allows users to generate personalized playlists from text prompts describing genres, moods, artists, activities, or creative ideas like animals, movie characters, colors, or emojis. Users can refine playlists with additional instructions, such as "more upbeat" or "happier songs". As of 24 April 2025, the feature was available in markets including Antigua and Barbuda, Australia, Bahamas, Barbados, Belize, Botswana, Burundi, Canada, Curaçao, Dominica, Eswatini, Fiji, Ghana, Grenada, Guyana, Ireland, Jamaica, Kenya, Kiribati, Lesotho, Liberia, Malawi, Malta, Marshall Islands, Namibia, Nauru, New Zealand, Nigeria, Palau, Papua New Guinea, Philippines, Rwanda, Saint Kitts and Nevis, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Samoa, Sierra Leone, Singapore, South Africa, Solomon Islands, Tanzania, Tonga, Uganda, United Kingdom, United States, Vanuatu, Zambia, and Zimbabwe.[[314]](#cite_note-317)[[315]](#cite_note-318)

### Listening limitations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Listening limitations")]

Spotify has experimented with different limitations to users' listening on the Free service tier.

In April 2011, Spotify announced via a blog post that they would drastically cut the amount of music that free members could access, effective 1 May 2011. The post stated that all free members would be limited to ten hours of music streaming per month, and in addition, individual tracks were limited to five plays. New users were exempt from these changes for six months.[[316]](#cite_note-319) In March 2013, the five-play individual track limit was removed for users in the United Kingdom, and media reports stated that users in the United States, Australia, and New Zealand never had the limit in the first place.[[317]](#cite_note-320)[[318]](#cite_note-321)

In December 2013, CEO Daniel Ek announced that Android and iOS smartphone users with the free service tier could listen to music in Shuffle mode, a feature in which users can stream music by specific artists and playlists without being able to pick which songs to hear. Mobile listening previously was not allowed in Spotify Free accounts. Ek stated that "We're giving people the best free music experience in the history of the smartphone."[[319]](#cite_note-322)[[320]](#cite_note-323) This limitation does not apply to Android and iOS tablets, or computers.

In January 2014, Spotify removed all time limits for Free users on all platforms, including on computers, which previously had a 10-hour monthly listening limit after a 6-month grace period.[[321]](#cite_note-324)[[322]](#cite_note-325)

In April 2018, Spotify began to allow Free users to listen on-demand to whatever songs they want for an unlimited number of times, as long as the song is on one of the user's 15 personalized discovery playlists.[[323]](#cite_note-326)

Before May 2020, all service users were limited to 10,000 songs in their library, after which they would receive an "Epic collection, friend" notification and would not be able to save more music to their library.[[324]](#cite_note-verge-limit-327) Spotify later removed this limit.[[324]](#cite_note-verge-limit-327)

| FLAC (lossless, 24-bit/44.1 kHz) |
| Podcasts | |
| HE-AAC v2 24 kbit/s | AAC 128 kbit/s |
| Vorbis 96 kbit/s |

Spotify is [proprietary](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software") and uses [digital rights management](/wiki/Digital_rights_management "Digital rights management") (DRM) controls.[[326]](#cite_note-DRM-329) Spotify's [terms and conditions](/wiki/Terms_and_conditions "Terms and conditions") do not permit users to [reverse-engineer](/wiki/Reverse_engineering "Reverse engineering") the application.

Spotify allows users to add local audio files for music not in its catalog into the user's library through Spotify's desktop application, and then allows users to synchronize those music files to Spotify's mobile apps or other computers over the same [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi") network as the primary computer by creating a Spotify playlist, and adding those local audio files to the playlist. Audio files must either be in the .mp3, .mp4 (.mp4 files that have video streams are not supported), or .m4p media formats. This feature is available only for Premium subscribers.

Spotify has a median playback latency of 265 ms (including local [cache](/wiki/Cache_(computing) "Cache (computing)")).[[327]](#cite_note-330)

In April 2014, Spotify moved away from the [peer-to-peer](/wiki/Peer-to-peer "Peer-to-peer") (P2P) system they had used to distribute music to users. Previously, a desktop user would listen to music from one of three sources: a cached file on the computer, one of Spotify's servers, or from other subscribers through the P2P system. P2P, a well-established Internet distribution system, served as an alternative that reduced Spotify's server resources and costs. However, Spotify ended the P2P setup in 2014, with Spotify's Alison Bonny telling [TorrentFreak](/wiki/TorrentFreak "TorrentFreak"): "We're gradually phasing out the use of our desktop P2P technology which has helped our users enjoy their music both speedily and seamlessly. We're now at a stage where we can power music delivery through our growing number of servers and ensure our users continue to receive a best-in-class service."[[328]](#cite_note-331)

Originally, Spotify had their own servers; however, in 2016, most of their infrastructure was migrated to [Google Cloud](/wiki/Google_Cloud_Platform "Google Cloud Platform").[[329]](#cite_note-332)

### Car Thing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Car Thing")]

Main article: [Car Thing](/wiki/Car_Thing "Car Thing")

Spotify first announced a voice-activated music-streaming gadget for cars in May 2019. Named the [Car Thing](/wiki/Car_Thing "Car Thing"), it represents the music-streaming service's first entry into hardware devices.[[330]](#cite_note-333) In early 2020, as part of filings to the Federal Communications Commission (FCC), submitted images of the device that make it seem much more like a miniature infotainment screen.[[331]](#cite_note-334) In April 2021, Spotify rolled out its own voice assistant with the hands-free wake word: "Hey Spotify".[[332]](#cite_note-335) Using this, users can perform various actions such as pulling playlists, launching radio stations, playing or pausing songs. This voice-based virtual assistant may be intended more towards Spotify's own hardware such as its "Car Thing".[[333]](#cite_note-336) The company discontinued the device in July 2022.[[334]](#cite_note-337) In May 2024 Spotify sent out announcements to Car Thing owners, stating that the hardware would fully stop working on 9 December 2024.[[335]](#cite_note-338)[[336]](#cite_note-339)

### Security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Security")]

Ahead of Spotify Wrapped 2024, Spotify changed its API so that modded versions of the client would not work.[[337]](#cite_note-340)[[338]](#cite_note-341)

### Social media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Social media")]

Spotify had added [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media")-related features to its platform, especially to compete for ad revenue and to increase its media-related users.[[339]](#cite_note-342)[[340]](#cite_note-auto3-343) Spotify users, like on other social media websites, can share playlists, do direct [messaging](/wiki/Private_message "Private message"),[[341]](#cite_note-344) have a discovery feed,[[342]](#cite_note-Perez-345) comment on podcasts,[[340]](#cite_note-auto3-343)[[342]](#cite_note-Perez-345) share playlists including collaborative ones,[[343]](#cite_note-Admin-346) do polls and Q&As through podcasters,[[342]](#cite_note-Perez-345) follow friends and artists,[[342]](#cite_note-Perez-345) create artist profiles,[[340]](#cite_note-auto3-343) have a 'for you page',[[340]](#cite_note-auto3-343) and create a user profile.[[343]](#cite_note-Admin-346)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Doja_Cat_promo_image.jpg/250px-Doja_Cat_promo_image.jpg)](/wiki/File:Doja_Cat_promo_image.jpg)

Spotify increased Doja Cat's popularity.

Artists through Spotify can sell tickets to their music venue,[[340]](#cite_note-auto3-343) promote their merchandise,[[340]](#cite_note-auto3-343) add their videos as [stories](/wiki/Story_(social_media) "Story (social media)") through Spotify clips,[[342]](#cite_note-Perez-345) and promote new releases of their work through countdown pages.[[342]](#cite_note-Perez-345) Spotify also has a 'blend' feature in which two users' music preferences are mixed in a shared playlist.[[343]](#cite_note-Admin-346) Spotify users can also 'jam' sessions where users can create and contribute live music playing sessions with their Spotify friends.[[344]](#cite_note-347) Spotify Wrapped had generated two billion impressions worldwide, and more ninety million Spotify users share their Spotify Wrapped statistics online, especially through social media.[[345]](#cite_note-348)

Artists through social media platforms, especially [TikTok](/wiki/TikTok "TikTok"), often got their popularity boost through Spotify.[[346]](#cite_note-soundon.global-349) For example, [Lil Nas X](/wiki/Lil_Nas_X "Lil Nas X")'s [Old Town Road](/wiki/Old_Town_Road "Old Town Road") and [Doja Cat](/wiki/Doja_Cat "Doja Cat")'s [Say So](/wiki/Say_So "Say So") started their popularity through TikTok but through Spotify playlists had their popularity accelerated.[[346]](#cite_note-soundon.global-349) This furthering of Doja Cat's popularity through Spotify helped with Doja Cat's [Paint the Town Red](/wiki/Paint_the_Town_Red_(Doja_Cat_song) "Paint the Town Red (Doja Cat song)") on 21 August 2023 becoming the #1 song on Spotify. This, in turn, made it the first time that a female solo rap artist achieved such a top spot in Spotify.[[347]](#cite_note-350)

However, there has been some concern about the music [streaming](/wiki/Music_streaming_service "Music streaming service") platform's social media features. Among them is an increased lack of non-online social spaces, an increased lack of privacy from strangers, and a source of unbridled expression particularly with its media-related podcasts.[[340]](#cite_note-auto3-343)

## Geographic availability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Geographic availability")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Availability_of_Spotify_in_the_World.svg/960px-Availability_of_Spotify_in_the_World.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Availability_of_Spotify_in_the_World.svg)

Countries where Spotify is available (as of December 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit))

The company is incorporated in Luxembourg as **Spotify Technology S.A.**,[[348]](#cite_note-351) and headquartered in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), Sweden, with offices in 16 countries around the world.[[349]](#cite_note-352)[[350]](#cite_note-353)[[351]](#cite_note-354)

As of December 2022, Spotify is available in 184 markets across Europe, Africa, the Americas, Asia, and Oceania.[[6]](#cite_note-About_Spotify-7) Despite the extensive global coverage, the service remains unavailable in several countries and territories, including Afghanistan, British Indian Ocean Territory, Central African Republic, China, Cuba, Eritrea, Iran, Myanmar, North Korea, Russia, Somalia, South Sudan, Sudan, Syria, Turkmenistan, and Yemen. While Spotify's core music streaming service is accessible in all of its active markets, podcasting services are not available in Bangladesh, Belarus, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Ethiopia, Iraq, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Libya, Moldova, Pakistan, the Republic of the Congo, Sri Lanka, Tajikistan, Uganda, and Venezuela.[[352]](#cite_note-355)

Following the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine"), it temporarily closed its office in Russia[[353]](#cite_note-356) and indefinitely suspended all of its services in the country.[[354]](#cite_note-357) In 2023, it announced that it would leave Uruguay due to a copyright law.[[355]](#cite_note-358) However, it reversed its decision a few weeks later.[[356]](#cite_note-359)

## Accolades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Accolades")]

In September 2010, the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum") (WEF) selected Spotify as a Technology Pioneer for 2011.[[398]](#cite_note-401)[[399]](#cite_note-402)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Reception")]

Main article: [Criticism of Spotify](/wiki/Criticism_of_Spotify "Criticism of Spotify")

Spotify has attracted significant criticism since its 2008 launch.[[400]](#cite_note-Launch-403) The primary point of criticism centers around what artists, music creators, and the media have described as "unsustainable" compensation.[[401]](#cite_note-404) Unlike physical sales or legal downloads (both of which were the main medium of listening to music at the time), which pay artists a fixed amount per song or album sold, Spotify pays royalties based on their "market share": the number of streams for their songs as a proportion of total songs streamed on the service. Spotify distributes approximately 70% of its total revenue to rights-holders, who will then pay artists based on their individual agreements.[[182]](#cite_note-Spotify_business-184) Worldwide, 30,000 musicians have joined the organization UnionOfMusicians (UMAW).[[402]](#cite_note-auto2-405) UMAW organized protests in 31 cities in March 2021 and its campaign #JusticeAtSpotify is demanding more transparency and a compensation of one cent per stream.[[402]](#cite_note-auto2-405)

Spotify has been criticised by artists and producers including [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift") and [Thom Yorke](/wiki/Thom_Yorke "Thom Yorke"), who have argued that Spotify does not fairly compensate musicians, and both withdrew their music from the service.[[403]](#cite_note-406)[[404]](#cite_note-407) Their catalogues returned to the service in 2017.[[405]](#cite_note-408)[[406]](#cite_note-409) While the streaming music industry in general faces the same critique about inadequate payments,[[407]](#cite_note-410) Spotify, being the leading service,[[408]](#cite_note-411) faces particular scrutiny due to its free service tier, allowing users to listen to music for free, though with advertisements between tracks. The free service tier has led to a variety of major album releases being delayed or withdrawn from the service.[[409]](#cite_note-412)[[410]](#cite_note-413)[[411]](#cite_note-414) In response to the allegations about unfair compensation, Spotify claims that it is benefitting the industry by migrating users away from [unauthorized copying](/wiki/Copyright_infringement "Copyright infringement") and less monetized platforms to its free service tier, and then downgrades that service until they upgrade to paid accounts.[[182]](#cite_note-Spotify_business-184) A study has shown that record labels keep a high amount of the money earned from Spotify,[[412]](#cite_note-415)[[413]](#cite_note-416) and the CEO of [Merlin Network](/wiki/Merlin_Network "Merlin Network"), a representative body for over 10,000 independent labels, has also observed significant yearly growth rates in earnings from Spotify, while clarifying that Spotify pays labels, not artists.[[414]](#cite_note-417) In 2017, as part of its efforts to renegotiate license deals for an interest in [going public](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering"), Spotify announced that artists would be able to make albums temporarily exclusive to paid subscribers if the albums are part of [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group") or the Merlin Network.

In 2016, Spotify was criticized for allegedly making certain artists' music harder to find than others, as these artists would release their music to the rival streaming service [Apple Music](/wiki/Apple_Music "Apple Music") before releasing it to Spotify.[[415]](#cite_note-418)

In May 2018, Spotify attracted criticism for its "Hate Content & Hateful Conduct policy" that removed the music of [R. Kelly](/wiki/R._Kelly "R. Kelly") and [XXXTentacion](/wiki/XXXTentacion "XXXTentacion") from its editorial and algorithmic playlists because "When we look at promotion, we look at issues around hateful conduct, where you have an artist or another creator who has done something off-platform that is so particularly out of line with our values, egregious, in a way that it becomes something that we don't want to associate ourselves with." R. Kelly has faced accusations of sexual abuse, while XXXTentacion was on trial for domestic abuse in a case that did not reach a judgement before [his death](/wiki/Killing_of_XXXTentacion "Killing of XXXTentacion") that June.[[416]](#cite_note-419) This policy was revoked in June because the company deemed the original wording to be too "vague"; they stated that "Across all genres, our role is not to regulate artists. Therefore, we are moving away from implementing a policy around artist conduct".[[417]](#cite_note-420) However, artists such as [Gary Glitter](/wiki/Gary_Glitter "Gary Glitter") and [Lostprophets](/wiki/Lostprophets "Lostprophets") are still hidden from Spotify's radio stations.[[418]](#cite_note-421)

According to some computer science and music experts, various music communities are often ignored or overlooked by music streaming services such as Spotify.[[419]](#cite_note-Rolling_Stone_2019-422)[[420]](#cite_note-Hepworth_2020-423)[[421]](#cite_note-RouteNote_Blog_2020-424) The most commonly perceived error is said to be caused by a lack of diverse scope within curation staff, including overlooking mainstay artists in large genres, potentially causing a categorical homogenization of musical styles; even impacting mainline artists like within [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") with [A Tribe Called Quest](/wiki/A_Tribe_Called_Quest "A Tribe Called Quest").[[420]](#cite_note-Hepworth_2020-423)

In March 2021, [David Dayen](/wiki/David_Dayen "David Dayen") argued in *The American Prospect* that musicians were in peril due to monopolies in streaming services like Spotify.[[422]](#cite_note-prospect.org-425) Daniel Ek, co-founder and CEO of Spotify, discussed "what he called an artist-friendly streaming solution". He explained, "An extension of the internet radio craze of the early 2000s, Spotify would license content from record labels, and then support artists as people listened to their music."[[422]](#cite_note-prospect.org-425)

In January 2022, 270 scientists, physicians, professors, doctors, and healthcare workers wrote an open letter to Spotify expressing concern over "false and societally harmful assertions" on [Joe Rogan](/wiki/Joe_Rogan "Joe Rogan")'s podcast, *[The Joe Rogan Experience](/wiki/The_Joe_Rogan_Experience "The Joe Rogan Experience")*, and asked Spotify to "establish a clear and public policy to moderate misinformation on its platform". The 270 signatories objected to Rogan broadcasting [COVID-19 misinformation](/wiki/COVID-19_misinformation "COVID-19 misinformation"), citing "a highly controversial" episode featuring guest [Robert Malone](/wiki/Robert_W._Malone "Robert W. Malone") (#1757).[[423]](#cite_note-426)[[424]](#cite_note-427) On 26 January, [Neil Young](/wiki/Neil_Young "Neil Young") removed his music from Spotify after they refused to remove the podcast.[[425]](#cite_note-428) Other artists and podcasters, such as [Joni Mitchell](/wiki/Joni_Mitchell "Joni Mitchell"), [Nils Lofgren](/wiki/Nils_Lofgren "Nils Lofgren"), [Brené Brown](/wiki/Bren%C3%A9_Brown "Brené Brown"), and [Crosby, Stills, & Nash](/wiki/Crosby,_Stills,_Nash_%26_Young "Crosby, Stills, Nash & Young"), also announced a boycott of Spotify.[[426]](#cite_note-429)[[427]](#cite_note-430)[[428]](#cite_note-431)[[429]](#cite_note-432) Spotify promised to add content advisories for anything containing discussions related to COVID-19 and posted additional rules.[[430]](#cite_note-433)

In 2024, Spotify Wrapped, which has become a key marketing strategy for the company, was criticised for appearing to rely on AI-generated content and producing uninteresting insights.[[431]](#cite_note-434)

In July 2025, several artists joined a boycott of Spotify because Spotify CEO and co-founder Daniel Ek raised another $600 million in support of German defense company [Helsing](/wiki/Helsing_(company) "Helsing (company)") through his investment fund. Helsing is an AI defense software company that also engages in military strike drone manufacturing.[[432]](#cite_note-435)

### Fake artists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Fake artists")]

Main article: [Controversy over fake artists on Spotify](/wiki/Controversy_over_fake_artists_on_Spotify "Controversy over fake artists on Spotify")

In 2021, a conspiracy theory began to take hold that Spotify was filling its playlists in some genres (including jazz, chill and "peaceful piano") with stock music attributed to a handful of little-known musicians, mostly Swedish, in an effort to meet demand for background music. In 2022, an investigation by the Swedish daily *[Dagens Nyheter](/wiki/Dagens_Nyheter "Dagens Nyheter")* found that around twenty songwriters were behind the work of more than five hundred "artists", and that thousands of their tracks were on Spotify and had been streamed millions of times, benefitting those artists versus other artists who might otherwise have supplied the background or ambient music. In the 2025 book *[Mood Machine: The Rise of Spotify and the Costs of the Perfect Playlist](/wiki/Mood_Machine:_The_Rise_of_Spotify_and_the_Costs_of_the_Perfect_Playlist "Mood Machine: The Rise of Spotify and the Costs of the Perfect Playlist")*, journalist Liz Pelly speculated that Spotify allowed these artists to put their music on Spotify. In doing so, if users chose to listen to one set of these artists' music, it effectively reduced the number of plays for other musicians. Pelly advocated for a move away from the current royalty model that says the artists who people listen to the most should make the most money toward a more generalized approach where the decision for who gets paid rests with industry or government bodies, not audiences.[[433]](#cite_note-436)[[434]](#cite_note-437)

### Privacy concerns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spotify&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Privacy concerns")]

Spotify does not sell user data but did begin selling broader trend data, often coined "streaming intelligence" to marketing firms in 2016, allowing for the data to be available directly to clients.[[19]](#cite_note-:3-20) According to journalist and author Liz Pelly, Spotify, "under the guise of AI-powered recommendations has developed a surveillance apparatus driven by emotion profiling and pseudoscience."[[19]](#cite_note-:3-20)

In July 2025, a leak containing the listening data of several politicians, journalists, and businesspeople, titled the Panama Playlists, was released online.[[435]](#cite_note-438)[[436]](#cite_note-439)
